evidence: src/lib/ is cleanly decomposed into components/, data/, types/, and utils/ directories, each with a clear single responsibility
src/routes/ follows SvelteKit conventions exactly: +layout.ts for SSR config, +layout.svelte for chrome, +page.server.ts for data loading, +page.svelte for composition
Data layer (src/lib/data/) exports typed arrays consumed by the page; types layer (src/lib/types/) defines shared interfaces; utils layer (src/lib/utils/) handles external API integration
The public surface is minimal: data modules export named constants, types module exports interfaces and one runtime constant, utils module exports fetch helpers and type guards
All project data is split into three semantic categories (impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects) reflecting domain distinctions rather than technical ones
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: The Artefact type uses a hardcoded GitHub owner URL in the Artefacts.svelte component rather than deriving it from the repo field like Project does, creating a subtle inconsistency in how the two parallel data models map to their presentation. The accentColors runtime constant lives in the types module, mixing runtime values with type definitions.

evidence: +page.server.ts fetches GitHub data via fetchAllRepoData, passing repo paths extracted from all three project arrays; clean composition with minimal boundary crossing
+page.svelte receives githubData via $props() and merges it with static project data through attachGithubData(), a single translation point at the data/presentation boundary
The data layer exports Omit<Project, 'github'>[] and the page adds the github field, creating a clear split: static data modules own content, the page owns the GitHub enrichment
Type guards (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) are co-located with the fetch functions that use them, keeping validation at the boundary where untrusted data enters
The Artefact data flow is simpler: artefacts array flows directly from data module to Artefacts.svelte component with no transformation needed
mid_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: The attachGithubData function in +page.svelte performs data merging inline in the presentation layer rather than in the server load function, meaning the Project type assembly happens client-side when it could be completed server-side. The Artefact component hardcodes the GitHub owner URL prefix rather than receiving it through the data contract.

evidence: 5 test files exist covering data modules (artefacts, projects, terminal), types, and github utils

github.test.ts tests the three pure exported functions (parseCommitCount, isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) with good edge case coverage including null, wrong types, multi-digit numbers, and missing fields
Data module tests (projects.test.ts, artefacts.test.ts, terminal.test.ts) verify structural invariants: non-empty arrays, required field presence, repo format validation, last-frame delay convention
No tests exist for the async fetch functions (fetchGitHub, fetchJson, fetchRepoData, fetchAllRepoData) which contain the actual integration logic with external API calls
+page.server.ts has zero test coverage; the scan evidence confirms it as an untested module
No integration tests verify the data flow from server load through to component rendering
No test infrastructure for Svelte components exists (no @testing-library/svelte or similar)

github.ts exports a coherent API surface: two type guard functions (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse), one pure parser (parseCommitCount), and two async fetch functions (fetchRepoData, fetchAllRepoData)
The naming convention is consistent: 'fetch' prefix for async data retrieval, 'is' prefix for type guards, 'parse' prefix for data transformation
fetchRepoData returns Promise<ProjectGitHubData | null> and fetchAllRepoData returns Promise<Record<string, ProjectGitHubData | null>>, both using null to represent failure consistently

The module mixes sync and async functions in its public API: isRepoResponse (sync), isLanguagesResponse (sync), parseCommitCount (sync), fetchRepoData (async), fetchAllRepoData (async), which is flagged by scan evidence
Internal helpers fetchGitHub and fetchJson are properly kept private (not exported)
src/lib/ is cleanly divided into components/ (8 Svelte files), data/ (3 data modules), types/ (1 index file), and utils/ (1 github helper) — each directory has a clear, single purpose
src/routes/ contains only SvelteKit-required files: +page.svelte, +page.server.ts, +layout.svelte, +layout.ts — no stray files
directory_profiles show src/lib/data/ has 3 files with avg_fan_in=1.0 and avg_fan_out=0.0, indicating cohesive leaf modules that export data without cross-dependencies
Total codebase is 7 production TS files across 288 LOC — small enough that the current flat-within-directory structure is proportionate
All imports follow clean paths: data files import from types/, page.server imports from utils/ and data/, components import from data/ and types/ — no circular or bidirectional coupling
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: The types/index.ts barrel file mixes type definitions (interfaces) with runtime values (accentColors constant and SectionAccent type). As the project grows, this conflation could cause consumers to import runtime code when they only need types, and makes it unclear whether types/ is a pure type-declaration directory or also a runtime module.

evidence: src/routes/+page.server.ts uses SvelteKit's load function pattern — all async work (GitHub API fetches) happens inside the load function, not at module scope
src/lib/utils/github.ts defines API_BASE and GITHUB_HEADERS as module-level constants, but these are pure string/object literals with no side effects
src/lib/data/ files (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) export only static data arrays — no import-time side effects, no I/O, no environment variable reads
src/routes/+layout.ts exports only two boolean constants (prerender=true, csr=true) — no side effects
No global singletons, no mutable module-level state, no environment variable reads at import time across the entire codebase
No circular import dependencies detected; all import paths are acyclic
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: The accentColors runtime constant in types/index.ts is evaluated at import time, though this is a pure data structure with no external dependencies. More significantly, the GitHub API fetch in +page.server.ts uses hardcoded URL constants rather than configuration, making it slightly harder to redirect API calls in testing contexts — though this is idiomatic for SvelteKit SSG.

evidence: src/routes/+page.server.ts is cleanly focused: it collects repo names from three project arrays and fetches GitHub data — single responsibility, 15 LOC
src/lib/utils/github.ts has well-decomposed functions: fetchGitHub (HTTP), fetchJson (parse), isRepoResponse (type guard), fetchRepoData (single repo), fetchAllRepoData (batch) — each does one thing

src/routes/+page.svelte mixes three concerns: (1) project data assembly with attachGithubData, (2) scroll-reveal animation via IntersectionObserver, (3) section tint color transitions via another IntersectionObserver — two distinct $effect blocks doing DOM manipulation

The attachGithubData function in +page.svelte merges static project data with server-fetched GitHub data — this is data transformation logic living in a view component rather than in a data/utility module
signal_density flags +page.server.ts with 2 detectors (orphaned, test_coverage), but 'orphaned' is a false positive for SvelteKit convention files
design_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: The main page component (+page.svelte) accumulates three distinct responsibilities: data transformation, scroll-reveal animation, and background tinting. While manageable at current scale, this mixing makes the component harder to reason about and test independently.

evidence: github.ts uses precise, descriptive function names: fetchGitHub, fetchJson, isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse, parseCommitCount, fetchRepoData, fetchAllRepoData — each name accurately predicts the function's behavior
Type names are clear and domain-appropriate: ProjectGitHubData, TerminalFrame, SectionAccent, Artefact
Data exports use descriptive category names: impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects — the prefix communicates the project category

fetchJson returns unknown (nullable via fetchGitHub returning null), but the name 'fetchJson' implies it returns parsed JSON — when fetchGitHub returns null, fetchJson also returns null, so the return type is actually 'unknown | null' though typed as Promise<unknown>

The variable name 'contribRaw' abbreviates 'contributors' inconsistently — sibling variables use 'repoRaw' and 'langRaw' where 'repo' and 'lang' are standard abbreviations, but 'contrib' is less established
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: Minor naming friction: fetchJson's name does not communicate that it can return null (unlike fetchGitHub which has a nullable return type annotation). The 'p' parameter name in lambda callbacks (projects.map((p) => p.repo)) is terse but acceptable for inline lambdas.

evidence: parseCommitCount receives 'responseOk' but the caller passes 'commitsRes !== null', conflating 'a response object exists' with 'the HTTP response was OK'; fetchGitHub already filters non-ok responses to null, so responseOk is always true when commitsRes is non-null, making the parameter redundant

isRepoResponse checks 'stargazers_count' and 'updated_at' types but does not check 'open_issues_count' type or 'language' field despite both being in the RepoResponse interface; the guard is incomplete but happens not to fail in practice
Data files (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) are simple typed constant arrays with no control flow; no logic clarity issues
+layout.ts exports two boolean constants with no branching; clean
+page.server.ts is a straightforward async load function with no branching

fetchJson declares return type Promise<unknown> but actually returns null (from the null check) or the parsed JSON; the return type is technically correct (null is unknown) but callers must narrow without guidance on what the possible shapes are
fetchGitHub declares Promise<Response | null> which accurately covers both paths; this is well-typed
isRepoResponse and isLanguagesResponse are proper user-defined type guards with correct 'data is X' return types
Data files use explicit typed exports (Artefact[], Omit<Project, 'github'>[], TerminalFrame[]) which is precise

The RepoResponse interface is defined locally in github.ts but the 'language' field (string | null) is never actually used after validation; it is part of the interface shape but fetchRepoData does not extract or return it
parseCommitCount has fully explicit parameter and return types
fetchRepoData returns Promise<ProjectGitHubData | null> and all code paths honor this: null on validation failure, full ProjectGitHubData object on success
fetchAllRepoData returns Promise<Record<string, ProjectGitHubData | null>> which accurately reflects that individual repo fetches may fail independently
isRepoResponse claims to validate RepoResponse but does not fully validate all fields (open_issues_count type, language type); the guard's contract is slightly broader than its implementation
Data exports (impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects) use Omit<Project, 'github'>[] which honestly communicates that GitHub data is not present at the data layer
parseCommitCount's parameter name 'responseOk' implies an HTTP status check but the caller passes a null-check result; the name promises more than the value delivers
+page.server.ts load function returns { githubData } matching SvelteKit PageServerLoad contract
fetchGitHub (line 6) uses try-catch and returns null on both network errors and non-ok responses, silently swallowing the error with no logging or context preservation
fetchJson (line 16) passes through null from fetchGitHub, adding another layer of silent failure; the original error cause (network failure vs 404 vs rate limit vs server error) is completely lost

fetchRepoData returns null on validation failure (line 63), which is consistent with fetchGitHub's null-on-failure pattern, but the consumer (+page.server.ts) receives null without knowing whether the failure was a network error, rate limit, or malformed response
The catch block in fetchGitHub (line 11) catches all errors indiscriminately; a DNS failure, a rate limit (429), and a server error (500) all produce the same null result
No error logging exists anywhere in the codebase; failures are entirely silent
The error strategy is internally consistent (always return null on failure) but context is systematically lost at every boundary
Types in src/lib/types/index.ts are directly consumed by components and data files with no unnecessary intermediate layers
fetchGitHub and fetchJson in github.ts are small helpers that each add behavior (error handling, JSON parsing) rather than pass-through wrappers
isRepoResponse and isLanguagesResponse are runtime type guards that earn their existence by validating unknown API data
The Omit<Project, 'github'> pattern in projects.ts is a precise type narrowing that avoids duplicating the Project interface
No single-implementation interfaces exist; all interfaces (Project, Artefact, TerminalFrame, ProjectGitHubData) are used directly by multiple consumers
Component props use Svelte 5 interface Props pattern consistently, which is framework-required, not ceremony
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: The accentColors record and SectionAccent type live in types/index.ts alongside pure data interfaces; this mixes runtime values with type definitions. The langColors record in ProjectCard.svelte duplicates color mapping logic that partially overlaps with accentColors.

evidence: Zero restating comments across the entire codebase; the only comments are structural HTML comments in Terminal.svelte ('Title bar', 'Terminal content') and a regex-behavior comment in ProjectCard.svelte, both of which add navigation value
No debug logging anywhere in the codebase; console.log is entirely absent
No docstring bloat; functions are uncommented but self-documenting through naming
No generic names like handleData or processItem; domain-specific names used throughout (fetchRepoData, parseCommitCount, attachGithubData, splitOverview, getLanguageSegments)
No identical boilerplate error handling; the try-catch in fetchGitHub is the only error handler and exists at a genuine network I/O boundary
The codebase comment ratio is 0.0 per the scan evidence, confirming no comment noise
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: The project descriptions in projects.ts read as LLM-generated prose: long, multi-sentence descriptions with a consistent rhetorical pattern (technical description followed by an interpretive gloss). While this is content data rather than code logic, the uniform voice across all 12 project descriptions is a subtle AI-debt signal.

evidence: fetchRepoData in github.ts uses Promise.all for parallel fetches followed by sequential type guard validation, which is direct and efficient
parseCommitCount is a clean extraction: takes raw header data and returns a number with clear fallback logic
splitOverview in ProjectCard.svelte uses regex to find sentence boundaries with a clean fallback when no match is found
The two $effect blocks in +page.svelte both follow the same pattern (create observer, observe elements, return cleanup) but handle different concerns (reveal animation vs background tint)
playFrames in Terminal.svelte uses a straightforward async loop with await for delays, which is simple and readable
getLanguageSegments has a minor extraction balance issue: langColors is defined at module scope but only used inside this one function
low_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: Two $effect blocks in +page.svelte are structurally similar (IntersectionObserver setup/teardown) but not extracted into a shared helper, creating mild duplication. The fetchRepoData function makes a sequential commits fetch after the parallel batch, which could be folded into the Promise.all for consistency.

evidence: Dependency direction is consistently unidirectional: routes import from lib/components and lib/data, components import from lib/types, data files import from lib/types, utils import from lib/types. No reverse dependencies.
No circular dependencies exist: the import graph is a clean DAG with types at the leaf, data and utils at the middle layer, and routes/components at the top
No hub modules: the most-imported file (src/lib/types/index.ts) has only 5 importers, which is appropriate for a types module in a 7-file codebase
No shared mutable state or import-time side effects: all modules export pure data or pure functions
No cross-module coordination through global state; GitHub data flows from +page.server.ts through page props to components
The coupling_matrix is empty per the blind packet, confirming no cross-directory boundary issues
No deferred imports or cycle pressure indicators in the blind packet
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: +page.svelte re-imports project data arrays (impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects) from lib/data/projects that were already used in +page.server.ts to determine which repos to fetch. This creates a subtle coupling: if someone adds a project to the data file, both the server load function and the page component automatically pick it up (which is correct), but the page component also independently needs to know the category grouping to render sections. The data flow would be cleaner if the server load function returned categorized projects rather than a flat githubData map.

evidence: Terminal.svelte uses onMount() for lifecycle while +page.svelte uses $effect() runes for equivalent IntersectionObserver setup; these are sibling-level components following different lifecycle protocols
All data modules in src/lib/data/ follow a consistent pattern: import type, export const with typed array
All components use consistent Props interface pattern with $props() destructuring
Naming is consistently camelCase for functions/variables across all TypeScript files
Export pattern is consistently named exports across all data modules and utility modules
package.json contains only framework-essential dependencies: SvelteKit, Svelte, Tailwind CSS, Vite, TypeScript, and Vitest
All listed dependencies are devDependencies; there are zero production dependencies
No duplicate-purpose libraries: single HTTP client (native fetch), single build tool (Vite), single test runner (Vitest)
No heavy utility libraries like lodash; the codebase uses native JS APIs throughout
Import graph shows no circular dependencies; all imports flow cleanly from routes -> lib/data, routes -> lib/utils, lib/data -> lib/types, lib/utils -> lib/types
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Version ranges use caret (^) for all dependencies, which is standard but means major version bumps could theoretically cause issues; however this is normal practice and not a real concern for this project size.

evidence: This is a static portfolio site with no authentication, no user accounts, no protected routes, and no server-side endpoints beyond the build-time data loader
The only server-side code is +page.server.ts which fetches public GitHub API data at build time with no auth tokens
src/lib/utils/github.ts makes unauthenticated requests to the public GitHub REST API, consistent with the documented design ('no auth, public repos only')
+layout.ts sets prerender=true and csr=true, confirming this is a fully static site with no runtime server
No route handlers, middleware, or API endpoints exist that would require authorization checks
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The dimension is not materially applicable to this codebase; the score reflects that there are no authorization concerns because the architecture intentionally has no protected resources.

evidence: All source files are TypeScript (.ts) or Svelte (.svelte) with lang='ts'; no .js source files exist, indicating a complete TypeScript migration
No TODO, FIXME, DEPRECATED, or legacy migration markers found anywhere in the codebase
No deprecated Svelte 4 API patterns like createEventDispatcher or export let for props; components use Svelte 5 $props() runes consistently
No mixed old/new API patterns: single HTTP client (fetch), single framework version, single styling approach (Tailwind 4 + CSS custom properties)
Terminal.svelte uses onMount from svelte, which is still a valid Svelte 5 API but represents an older pattern compared to the $effect rune used elsewhere
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: Terminal.svelte retains onMount() while sibling code uses $effect() for equivalent patterns; this is a minor old-pattern residue rather than a true migration gap, since onMount remains a supported API.

strengths: Directory structure maps precisely to domain capabilities: data (content), types (contracts), utils (external integration), components (presentation), routes (composition)
A new engineer could explain every top-level directory's purpose within minutes; there are no historical accidents or ambiguous folders
Public exports are small and consistent: data modules export typed arrays, types module exports interfaces, utils module exports functions
Single-page architecture means the composition point (+page.svelte) is the only orchestration site, making change boundaries predictable
SvelteKit conventions are followed without deviation, so framework knowledge transfers directly
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are minor boundary-clarity concerns rather than structural decomposition problems; the codebase is small enough that even these are low-friction in practice.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase exhibits clear, domain-aligned decomposition with predictable ownership boundaries. A developer can explain why every directory and file exists, and the public surface is minimal and consistent. The score sits above 85 because the architecture is genuinely well-structured for its scale, but below 90 because the types module mixes runtime values with type definitions and the Artefact/Project models have a subtle inconsistency in how they reference GitHub URLs. At 288 LOC across 7 production files, this is a codebase where architectural elegance is easy to achieve but has been achieved deliberately rather than by accident.

strengths: Data translation at the GitHub API boundary happens in exactly one place: github.ts type guards validate, fetchRepoData transforms to ProjectGitHubData, and the result flows upward
The Omit<Project, 'github'> pattern in data modules makes the handoff contract explicit: static data does not carry GitHub fields, the page composes them
Error propagation from GitHub API follows a consistent null-return pattern: fetchGitHub returns null on failure, fetchRepoData returns null, and the page gracefully handles null github fields
Orchestration in +page.server.ts reads as clean composition: collect repos, fetch all data, return result
Component props interfaces are explicit and minimal, using Svelte 5 $props() pattern consistently
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are about where data assembly happens (presentation vs server) rather than about tangled or unclear handoffs; the seams exist and are clean, but one transformation could be better placed.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The handoff design between data modules, server load, and page composition is generally well-structured. The GitHub data enrichment flow is explicit and the boundary between static content and dynamic API data is clear. The score sits at 85.5 because the seams are clean and predictable, but the attachGithubData transformation happening in the page component rather than the server load function is a misplaced boundary concern. The Artefacts component hardcoding the GitHub owner URL is a minor seam inconsistency compared to how ProjectCard derives its URL from the repo field. These are refinement opportunities rather than structural problems.

strengths: Pure function testing in github.test.ts is thorough: parseCommitCount covers valid link headers, missing headers, non-last pages, and multi-digit page numbers
Type guard tests (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) verify both positive and negative cases including null, missing fields, and wrong value types
Data module tests enforce meaningful structural contracts rather than just snapshot comparisons: repo format validation, required field checks, terminal frame delay conventions
Tests use Vitest with clean describe/it structure and no unnecessary mocking
test_strategy.issue_character: The test strategy covers the easy-to-test pure functions and static data but leaves the critical async integration paths (GitHub API fetching, server-side data loading, data merging) entirely unvalidated, creating a significant confidence gap for the code paths most likely to break.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The existing tests are well-written and cover the pure function layer effectively. However, the async fetch functions in github.ts (fetchGitHub, fetchJson, fetchRepoData, fetchAllRepoData) represent the most complex and failure-prone code in the codebase and have zero test coverage. The server load function in +page.server.ts is also untested. A developer making changes to the GitHub integration or data loading pipeline would have no test safety net. This places the score at 62: the test strategy has meaningful gaps in the areas where tests would provide the most value, even though the existing tests are good quality.

strengths: Consistent naming vocabulary: fetch* for async data retrieval, is* for type guards, parse* for transformation; a developer can predict function behavior from the name prefix
Error contract is uniform: all fetch functions return null on failure, type guards return boolean, parser returns number; no mixed throw/return-null within the same concern
Parameter shapes are minimal and unsurprising: fetchRepoData takes a string repo path, fetchAllRepoData takes string[], parseCommitCount takes a nullable string and boolean
The public API is small (5 exports) with clear separation between validation utilities and data fetching
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The mixed sync/async exports are a minor coherence concern but justified by the nature of the functions (type guards are inherently synchronous); the more substantive issue is that the sync utility functions are exported primarily for testability rather than as a deliberate public API for external consumers.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The API surface is well-structured with consistent naming, predictable parameter shapes, and a uniform error contract. A developer seeing fetchRepoData can predict what fetchAllRepoData does. The sync/async mix is flagged by the scanner but is justified: type guards and parsers are inherently synchronous while data fetching is inherently async, so mixing them in a single module is the natural choice. The score sits at 82 because the sync utilities (parseCommitCount, isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) are exported for testability but are implementation details that external consumers should not need to call, slightly widening the API surface beyond its natural boundary.

strengths: Clear four-way split under src/lib/ (components, data, types, utils) with each directory serving a distinct purpose
No straggler root-level files — all files live in appropriate directories
Import directions are consistently downward: routes -> lib/utils, routes -> lib/data, lib/data -> lib/types, lib/utils -> lib/types
Directory naming is unambiguous — a new developer can predict contents from names alone
package_organization.issue_character: The only organizational concern is a minor type/runtime mixing issue in a single file; the codebase is otherwise well-organized for its scale.
package_organization.score_rationale: At 7 production files and 288 LOC, this codebase is well within the range where the current directory structure is proportionate and navigable. The four-directory split under src/lib/ provides clear ownership boundaries. The only friction point is the mixing of runtime values into types/index.ts, which prevents a perfect score but does not meaningfully impede navigation today. Score of 90 reflects strong organization with one minor structural impurity.

strengths: All async I/O is confined to SvelteKit's load() function — no network calls at module scope
Module-level constants are all pure literals (strings, static arrays, objects) with zero external dependencies
No environment variable reads at import time anywhere in the codebase
No mutable global singletons or shared state that could create boot-order dependencies
Import graph is fully acyclic with clean unidirectional flow
initialization_coupling.issue_character: No initialization coupling issues were found; the codebase is essentially free of boot-order dependencies.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This codebase demonstrates strong initialization hygiene. All I/O is properly deferred to SvelteKit's load lifecycle, module-level code consists entirely of pure data declarations, and there are no singletons or environment reads at import time. The score of 93 reflects near-exemplary practice, held back only slightly by the hardcoded API constants that make test-time redirection marginally harder — a very minor concern in a static site context.

strengths: github.ts demonstrates clean functional decomposition — each function has a single purpose and they compose naturally (fetchGitHub -> fetchJson -> fetchRepoData -> fetchAllRepoData)
Data modules (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) are pure data declarations with no logic — appropriately simple
Type guards (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) are extracted as standalone, testable functions rather than inline type assertions
+page.server.ts is minimal and focused — collects repos, fetches data, returns it
design_coherence.issue_character: The primary design concern is responsibility accumulation in the main page component, where data transformation and two distinct animation behaviors coexist in a single file.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The TypeScript modules demonstrate strong design coherence — functions are focused, abstractions are earned, and the decomposition in github.ts is particularly clean. The score is pulled down from 90+ by the +page.svelte component accumulating three responsibilities (data merging, reveal animations, tint transitions) that would benefit from extraction. At 85, this reflects a codebase that is 'solid but with one uneven spot' — the backend/data layer is well-designed while the view layer shows some concern mixing.

strengths: github.ts function names form a readable progression: fetchGitHub (raw HTTP) -> fetchJson (parsed) -> fetchRepoData (single) -> fetchAllRepoData (batch) — naming reveals the abstraction layer
Type guard functions follow the 'is' prefix convention consistently: isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse
Data file exports clearly communicate their content category: impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects, irisSession, artefacts
Interface field names use domain-appropriate vocabulary: commitCount, lastUpdated, stargazers_count (mirroring GitHub API), techLine
naming_quality.issue_character: Naming issues are minor and isolated — a few names that slightly obscure null-return behavior or use inconsistent abbreviation, but no systemic naming problems.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates strong naming discipline overall. Function names predict behavior accurately, type names communicate their domain, and data exports use clear categorical prefixes. The score of 88 reflects that a developer can mostly read names and correctly predict behavior. It falls short of 90+ because fetchJson's name does not communicate its nullable return semantics, and there is a minor abbreviation inconsistency (contribRaw vs the more standard repoRaw/langRaw pattern).

strengths: Control flow in fetchRepoData follows a clear linear pattern: fetch in parallel, validate, transform, return
Guard clauses (if (!isRepoResponse(repoRaw)) return null) are used consistently for early exit
No dead code paths, no identical branches, no always-true conditions in any file
The codebase is small enough that all logic paths are easily traceable
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are isolated to the github.ts utility module and involve minor parameter semantics and incomplete type guard coverage rather than structural logic problems.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase is small and its control flow is generally direct and intention-revealing. The parseCommitCount parameter semantics are slightly misleading, and the type guard incompleteness is a minor gap. These prevent a score in the 90s but the overall logic clarity is solid. Against the global anchors, a developer can trace logic without surprises in almost all cases, placing this in the low 80s.

strengths: All public API functions in github.ts have explicit return type annotations
User-defined type guards (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) correctly narrow unknown to typed shapes
Data modules use precise types from the shared types/index.ts, including Omit<Project, 'github'> which accurately reflects the partial shape before GitHub data enrichment
TypeScript strict mode is enabled per project configuration
The types/index.ts file provides clean, minimal interfaces with appropriate optionality (liveUrl?: string)
type_safety.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in the github.ts utility module where the boundary between untyped API responses and typed internal models creates opportunities for type gaps; the rest of the codebase is well-typed by construction.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase uses TypeScript strict mode and most types are accurate. However, fetchJson's return type of Promise<unknown> pushes all narrowing responsibility to callers without any typed alternative, and the RepoResponse interface includes a field (language) that is validated but never used. The data layer and types are precise. Against global anchors, a developer would mostly trust types but would encounter a few spots in github.ts where annotations do not fully document runtime behavior, placing this around 80 but slightly below due to the fetchJson gap.

strengths: Return types across all functions accurately reflect their possible outputs including null/failure cases
The Omit<Project, 'github'> pattern explicitly communicates the contract boundary between static data and runtime-enriched data
Type guard functions (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) follow the standard TypeScript narrowing contract pattern
SvelteKit conventions (+page.server.ts load, +layout.ts exports) are followed correctly
contract_coherence.issue_character: Issues are minor contract mismatches within github.ts: a type guard that does not fully validate its claimed shape, and a parameter name that overpromises relative to what callers actually pass.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Functions generally honor their stated contracts. Return types match actual return paths, and the data layer's use of Omit honestly signals the shape boundary. The type guard incompleteness and parameter naming in parseCommitCount are real but minor contract gaps. Against global anchors, a developer would mostly trust that APIs do what they promise, with occasional need to verify details in github.ts. This places the score solidly in the low-to-mid 80s.

strengths: The return-null strategy is applied consistently across all functions in github.ts; a developer can predict the failure mode
fetchAllRepoData preserves per-repo failure granularity by storing null per key rather than failing the entire batch
The design philosophy (GitHub data is supplementary per CLAUDE.md) justifies graceful degradation rather than hard failures
error_consistency.issue_character: The issues are systemic rather than isolated: the entire GitHub API layer uses a uniform return-null strategy that consistently discards error context, making it impossible to distinguish between transient failures (rate limits, network issues) and permanent ones (invalid repo paths, API changes).
error_consistency.score_rationale: The error strategy is consistent (always null) which is better than mixed strategies, but the complete absence of error context preservation means debugging failures requires adding logging or checking the GitHub API directly. The broad catch in fetchGitHub treats all failure modes identically, which is a significant gap for an I/O boundary. The CLAUDE.md design intent (GitHub data is supplementary) provides some justification for graceful degradation, but even supplementary data benefits from distinguishing 'API is down' from 'repo does not exist'. This places the score around 75: consistent strategy with meaningful context loss.

strengths: Zero pass-through wrappers: every function in the codebase adds behavior, validation, or data transformation
Type guards (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) earn their existence by validating unknown external data at the API boundary
Omit<Project, 'github'> precisely narrows the Project type for static data files without duplicating the interface
No single-implementation interface ecosystem; all interfaces serve multiple concrete consumers across components and data modules
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Issues are minor and localized: a runtime value (accentColors) co-located with pure type definitions, and a minor color mapping overlap between types/index.ts and ProjectCard.svelte.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates strong abstraction fitness with no unnecessary indirection, no ceremony-only types, and no pass-through patterns. Every abstraction layer adds concrete value. The minor issue of runtime values mixed with type definitions and a small color mapping overlap prevent a higher score, but these are cosmetic rather than structural. This places the codebase solidly in the 90+ range per the global anchors.

strengths: Zero restating comments; the codebase achieves a 0.0 comment ratio without sacrificing clarity
No defensive overengineering: the try-catch in fetchGitHub wraps a genuine network call, not a pure expression
Domain-specific function names throughout (parseCommitCount, splitOverview, getLanguageSegments) with no generic handleData-style naming
No boilerplate error handling duplication; error strategy is concentrated in one utility module
No debug logging or console dumps anywhere in production code
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The only AI-debt signal is in content data (project description prose style), not in code structure, logic, or patterns.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase is remarkably clean from an AI-debt perspective. There are no restating comments, no defensive overengineering, no boilerplate duplication, and no generic naming. The code reads as purpose-driven throughout. The minor concern about uniform prose style in project descriptions is a content-level observation rather than a code-quality issue, keeping the score in the strong 90+ range per global anchors.

strengths: Guard clause pattern used consistently: isRepoResponse check exits early before proceeding with data assembly in fetchRepoData
Side effects in Terminal.svelte are explicit and bounded: IntersectionObserver created in onMount with cleanup returned
Helper extraction is well-calibrated: splitOverview, formatDate, getLanguageSegments are each focused single-purpose extractions
No deep nesting anywhere in the codebase; maximum indent depth is 3 levels in the IntersectionObserver callbacks
Edge case handling is precise: parseCommitCount handles both present and absent Link headers without defensive sprawl
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are minor structural inconsistencies: duplicated observer setup patterns in one file and a sequential fetch that could be parallel.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Function internals are generally direct and intention-revealing. Control flow is clean with good guard clause usage, and helper extraction is well-calibrated across the codebase. Two minor issues prevent a higher score: the duplicated IntersectionObserver pattern in +page.svelte and the sequential commits fetch in fetchRepoData that breaks the parallel-fetch pattern established by the surrounding code. These are isolated craft issues, not systemic problems, placing this solidly at 85.

strengths: Clean unidirectional dependency graph: types at leaf, data/utils at middle, routes/components at top, with no reverse imports
Zero circular dependencies confirmed by both code inspection and the empty coupling matrix in scan evidence
No shared mutable state: all cross-module data flows through explicit function parameters or Svelte props
GitHub data flows through a single well-defined path: +page.server.ts fetches, passes to +page.svelte via props, which distributes to ProjectCard components
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The single architectural concern is a mild data-flow duplication where project arrays are imported independently by both the server loader and the page component, creating parallel but consistent consumption paths.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase has exemplary dependency direction with a clean DAG, no cycles, no hub modules, and no shared mutable state. For a 7-file codebase this is straightforward to achieve, but it is nonetheless done correctly. The mild data-flow duplication between server and client imports of project arrays is the only concern, and it follows SvelteKit's intended architecture pattern. This places the score at 90: strong with a minor structural observation.

strengths: All three data modules (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) follow an identical pattern: type import, typed const export
Components consistently use the Svelte 5 Props interface + $props() destructuring pattern
Naming conventions are uniformly camelCase for functions and variables across all TypeScript modules
Export patterns are consistently named exports throughout; no mixed default/named exports
convention_outlier.issue_character: The single convention inconsistency is a lifecycle pattern split between onMount and $effect runes across sibling components, representing incomplete adoption of the newer Svelte 5 idiom.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is small and highly consistent in naming, file organization, export patterns, and component structure. The one notable drift is Terminal.svelte using onMount() while +page.svelte uses $effect() for the same kind of IntersectionObserver setup. This is a minor style island in an otherwise uniform codebase, pulling the score slightly below 85 but well above 80.

strengths: Zero production dependencies; the entire dependency set is devDependencies for build tooling
No duplicate-purpose libraries; native fetch is used for HTTP, no lodash or similar utility libraries
Clean, minimal dependency set with only framework-essential packages (SvelteKit, Svelte, Tailwind, Vite, TypeScript, Vitest)
No circular dependency cycles in the import graph
GitHub API integration uses native fetch rather than pulling in axios or another HTTP client
dependency_health.issue_character: No dependency health issues were found; the dependency set is lean and purposeful.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is exemplary for a project of this type. Every dependency serves a clear purpose, there is no duplication, no heavy libraries for light use, and no circular imports. The score reflects near-ideal dependency hygiene with only the most minor theoretical concern about caret version ranges preventing a perfect 100.

strengths: Architecture is intentionally public-only with no auth requirements, documented clearly in CLAUDE.md ('no auth, public repos only')
GitHub API calls are explicitly unauthenticated, matching the public-data-only design
Static site generation eliminates runtime authorization concerns entirely
No secrets or tokens in source code; API calls use no authentication headers beyond the standard Accept header
authorization_consistency.issue_character: No authorization issues exist because the codebase has no protected resources or authenticated endpoints by design.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This is a statically generated portfolio site with no authentication, no protected routes, and no user-facing API endpoints. The dimension is not materially applicable. The GitHub API integration is intentionally unauthenticated for public data only, which is clearly documented. Score reflects clean, intentional design rather than oversight.

strengths: Complete TypeScript adoption with zero .js source files
Consistent Svelte 5 runes adoption: $props(), $state(), $derived(), $effect() used throughout
No deprecated-but-called symbols or DEPRECATED markers anywhere in the codebase
No compatibility shims or legacy wrappers present
No stale migration TODOs or FIXMEs referencing legacy code
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The only trace of older patterns is Terminal.svelte using onMount() alongside the $effect() rune used in sibling files, which is a minor style residue rather than a blocking migration gap.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase shows a clean, complete adoption of its target stack: TypeScript throughout, Svelte 5 runes for state and props, Tailwind CSS 4, and modern SvelteKit conventions. The single onMount() usage in Terminal.svelte is a minor old-pattern residue that keeps the score below exemplary but does not cause confusion or dual-path problems. This places comfortably in the 'strong' range.

issues.summary: accentColors runtime constant exported from types/index.ts alongside pure type definitions
issues.suggestion: Move accentColors to a dedicated src/lib/data/theme.ts or src/lib/data/accents.ts module, keeping src/lib/types/index.ts as a pure type definition module. Update imports in Section.svelte and ProjectCard.svelte accordingly.
evidence: accentColors is a Record<SectionAccent, string> runtime constant defined at line 32-37 of src/lib/types/index.ts, which otherwise contains only interfaces and type aliases. This mixes runtime values with type-only definitions, blurring the module's role as a pure contract layer.

issues.summary: attachGithubData merges server data with static data in +page.svelte instead of +page.server.ts
issues.suggestion: Move the merge logic into +page.server.ts so it returns { impactProjects: Project[], explorationProjects: Project[], metaProjects: Project[] } with github data already attached. This keeps data assembly on the server side and simplifies the page component to pure presentation.
evidence: In +page.svelte lines 22-27, attachGithubData maps over static project arrays and attaches github data from the page's server-loaded data. This data assembly could happen in +page.server.ts where the GitHub data is fetched, returning fully assembled Project[] arrays. Currently the page component must know about the Omit<Project, 'github'> shape and the githubData record key structure.

issues.summary: Artefacts.svelte hardcodes GitHub owner URL prefix instead of deriving from data contract
issues.suggestion: Change artefacts.ts repo values to include the owner prefix (e.g., 'JasonWarrenUK/nihilistic-onboarder') and update Artefacts.svelte to use 'https://github.com/{artefact.repo}' for consistency with ProjectCard. This aligns both data models on the same repo path convention.
evidence: Artefacts.svelte line 25 constructs URLs as 'https://github.com/JasonWarrenUK/{artefact.repo}', hardcoding the owner. In contrast, ProjectCard.svelte line 123 uses 'https://github.com/{project.repo}' where the repo field already contains 'owner/repo'. The Artefact type's repo field contains only the repo name (e.g., 'nihilistic-onboarder'), while Project's repo field contains 'owner/repo' (e.g., 'JasonWarrenUK/sparker'). This inconsistency means the two parallel data paths use different URL construction strategies at the presentation boundary.

issues.summary: Async fetch functions in github.ts (fetchRepoData, fetchAllRepoData) have zero test coverage
issues.suggestion: Add tests for fetchRepoData and fetchAllRepoData using a mocked global fetch (vi.stubGlobal or vi.spyOn). Test cases should cover: successful fetch returning valid ProjectGitHubData, API returning non-ok status (should return null), network error (should return null), partial API failures (some repos succeed, some fail), and deduplication in fetchAllRepoData.
evidence: github.test.ts tests only the three pure exported functions: parseCommitCount, isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse. The async functions fetchGitHub (line 6), fetchJson (line 16), fetchRepoData (line 54), and fetchAllRepoData (line 84) are untested. These functions contain the core integration logic: HTTP requests, response parsing, error handling via null returns, Promise.all orchestration, and data assembly into ProjectGitHubData.

issues.summary: +page.server.ts load function has no test coverage for build-time data fetching
issues.suggestion: Add a test that mocks fetchAllRepoData and verifies the load function correctly aggregates repos from all three project arrays and passes them to fetchAllRepoData. This validates the wiring between static data and GitHub API integration.
evidence: The load function in +page.server.ts aggregates all repo paths from three project arrays and calls fetchAllRepoData. It is flagged by scan evidence as an untested module (15 LOC, 0 importers). While the function is simple, it is the single entry point for all build-time data fetching and any failure here would produce a broken static build.

issues.summary: Type guards and parseCommitCount are exported as public API but serve only as internal validation helpers
issues.suggestion: Consider testing these indirectly through fetchRepoData (with mocked fetch) rather than exporting them. If keeping them exported for direct testing is preferred, document this intent with a comment (e.g., '// Exported for testing') so future developers understand the export is deliberate rather than indicating these are part of the public contract.
evidence: isRepoResponse (line 29), isLanguagesResponse (line 41), and parseCommitCount (line 46) are exported from github.ts. They are only consumed by fetchRepoData internally and by github.test.ts for testing. No other module imports them. These functions are implementation details of the fetch pipeline, exposed as public API solely for testability, which widens the module's contract surface.

issues.summary: types/index.ts exports runtime value (accentColors) alongside type definitions
issues.suggestion: Move the accentColors constant and SectionAccent type to a new file src/lib/data/accents.ts or src/lib/theme.ts, keeping types/index.ts purely for interface/type exports. Staged plan: 1) Create src/lib/data/accents.ts with the SectionAccent type and accentColors export. 2) Update imports in any components that reference accentColors. 3) Verify with 'bun run build' that no import paths break.
evidence: src/lib/types/index.ts defines accentColors as a runtime Record<SectionAccent, string> constant alongside pure type/interface exports (ProjectGitHubData, Project, Artefact, TerminalFrame). The directory name 'types/' implies pure type declarations, but this file also serves as a runtime module.

issues.summary: +page.svelte mixes data transformation, reveal animation, and tint transition into one component
issues.suggestion: Extract the two animation behaviors into reusable Svelte actions or separate utility modules: 1) Create src/lib/utils/reveal.ts exporting a Svelte action for scroll-reveal behavior. 2) Create src/lib/utils/tint.ts exporting a Svelte action for background tint transitions, with sectionTints as a parameter. 3) Move attachGithubData to src/lib/utils/projects.ts or inline the merge in +page.server.ts so the page component receives ready-to-render data. This keeps +page.svelte as pure composition/layout.
evidence: attachGithubData() on line 22 is data transformation logic embedded in the view component
First $effect (line 40) handles scroll-reveal animation with IntersectionObserver
Second $effect (line 63) handles background tint transitions with a separate IntersectionObserver
sectionTints constant (line 29) is presentation configuration mixed into the component script

issues.summary: fetchJson name does not communicate that it returns null on HTTP failure
issues.suggestion: Rename to fetchJsonOrNull or tryFetchJson to communicate the nullable return semantics, matching the pattern where fetchGitHub already returns Promise<Response | null>. Alternatively, add the null to the return type annotation: Promise<unknown | null>.
evidence: fetchJson (line 16) returns Promise<unknown> but actually returns null when fetchGitHub returns null on HTTP failure. The name 'fetchJson' implies it fetches and parses JSON, but the null-on-failure behavior is hidden. Callers must know to check for null, which the name does not suggest.

issues.summary: parseCommitCount 'responseOk' parameter is always equivalent to 'linkHeader !== null || response exists'
issues.suggestion: Remove the responseOk parameter from parseCommitCount. The function only needs the linkHeader string. If no Link header match is found, return 1 (a valid response with no pagination means exactly 1 page of commits). The caller simplifies to parseCommitCount(commitsRes?.headers.get('Link') ?? null).
evidence: fetchGitHub returns null for non-ok responses, so when commitsRes is non-null, the response was ok. The caller passes 'commitsRes !== null' as responseOk, which is tautologically true when commitsRes exists (line 71). The fallback 'return responseOk ? 1 : 0' is reached only when linkHeader has no match, and at that point responseOk is always true because commitsRes was non-null to reach this code path.

issues.summary: isRepoResponse validates stargazers_count and updated_at types but not open_issues_count or language
issues.suggestion: Add typeof checks for open_issues_count (number) to match the interface. The language field is string | null so its check is less critical, but open_issues_count is used directly in the return value (line 80) and should be validated.
evidence: The RepoResponse interface declares open_issues_count: number and language: string | null, but isRepoResponse only checks typeof stargazers_count === 'number' and typeof updated_at === 'string'. The 'in' check confirms key presence but not value types for all fields.

issues.summary: fetchJson returns Promise<unknown> but can return null; callers get no type guidance on possible shapes
issues.suggestion: Change the return type to Promise<unknown | null> to explicitly signal the null case. Alternatively, consider making fetchJson generic with a type guard parameter: async function fetchJson<T>(url: string, guard: (data: unknown) => data is T): Promise<T | null> to push type narrowing into the fetch call.
evidence: fetchJson (line 16) returns Promise<unknown> which technically covers null, but callers must independently know what shapes to expect. The function returns null when fetchGitHub returns null, or the parsed JSON otherwise. A return type of Promise<unknown | null> would be more explicit, though the real improvement would be a generic overload or documenting the null case.

issues.summary: RepoResponse includes 'language' field that is validated by isRepoResponse but never read by fetchRepoData
issues.suggestion: Remove 'language' from the RepoResponse interface since it is not used by any consumer. If it is intended for future use, add a comment explaining the intent.
evidence: RepoResponse interface (line 22) declares language: string | null. isRepoResponse does not validate this field's type (only presence via 'in' check is absent for language). fetchRepoData (line 54) destructures/uses stargazers_count, open_issues_count, and updated_at but never accesses repo.language. The field exists in the interface but serves no purpose.

issues.summary: isRepoResponse claims to validate RepoResponse shape but does not verify all field types
issues.suggestion: Add typeof (data as RepoResponse).open_issues_count === 'number' to the guard. For language, check typeof === 'string' || value === null. This makes the guard's implementation match its stated contract.
evidence: isRepoResponse (line 29) returns 'data is RepoResponse' but only validates typeof for stargazers_count and updated_at. The RepoResponse interface also includes open_issues_count: number and language: string | null, neither of which has typeof validation. A caller trusting the type guard would assume all fields are type-safe, but open_issues_count could be a non-number value.

issues.summary: fetchGitHub catches all errors and returns null without logging or preserving error context
issues.suggestion: Add a console.warn in the catch block logging the URL and error message. Consider returning a discriminated result type like { ok: false, reason: 'network' | 'rate_limit' | 'not_found' | 'server_error' } instead of bare null, or at minimum log the HTTP status code for non-ok responses before returning null.
evidence: The catch block on line 11 catches any error and returns null. A rate limit (429), network failure, DNS error, and server error (500) all produce the same null result. No console.warn, no error categorization, no way for callers to distinguish failure modes. During build-time SSG, a rate-limited API call silently produces empty GitHub data with no build log evidence.

issues.summary: fetchGitHub conflates non-ok HTTP responses with network/parse exceptions into a single null
issues.suggestion: Separate the non-ok response path from the exception path. For non-ok responses, log the status code (res.status) and consider special handling for 403/429 (rate limit). For caught exceptions, log the error. Even if both ultimately return null for graceful degradation, the logging distinction aids debugging.
evidence: Lines 8-9 check res.ok and return null for non-ok responses. Lines 10-13 catch network exceptions and also return null. These are fundamentally different failure modes (the server responded with an error vs the request never completed) but produce identical results. A 403 rate limit could be retried after a delay; a network error might indicate a build environment issue.

issues.summary: accentColors runtime record exported from types/index.ts alongside pure type definitions
issues.suggestion: Move accentColors to a dedicated constants file (e.g., src/lib/data/constants.ts or src/lib/data/accents.ts) and keep types/index.ts purely for type definitions.
evidence: accentColors is a runtime Record<SectionAccent, string> value exported from the types module, which otherwise contains only interfaces and type aliases. This mixes concerns: consumers importing types also pull in runtime values.

issues.summary: Project descriptions in projects.ts follow a uniform rhetorical pattern suggesting AI-generated prose
issues.suggestion: Vary the description style across projects: some could be shorter and punchier, some could lead with the problem rather than the solution, and the interpretive glosses could be reserved for projects where they add genuine insight rather than applied uniformly.
evidence: All 12 project descriptions follow the same pattern: technical capability statement, followed by an architectural insight, ending with an interpretive gloss (e.g., 'Relationships as the primary data structure, not an afterthought', 'The process of building it is the transferable skill, not the specifics'). This consistent voice across diverse projects reads as AI-generated content.

issues.summary: Two $effect blocks in +page.svelte duplicate IntersectionObserver setup/teardown boilerplate
issues.suggestion: Extract a reusable helper like createSectionObserver(selector, options, onIntersect) that handles observer lifecycle, and call it from each $effect with different callbacks and options.
evidence: Lines 40-61 and 63-84 both create an IntersectionObserver, configure it with options, observe a set of elements, and return a cleanup function. The structure is identical; only the callback behavior and options differ.

issues.summary: fetchRepoData makes commits API call sequentially after the parallel Promise.all batch
issues.suggestion: Include the commits fetch in the Promise.all call: add fetchGitHub(`${repoUrl}/commits?per_page=1`) as a fourth element, then destructure and process the commits response alongside the others.
evidence: Lines 57-61 use Promise.all to fetch repo, languages, and contributors in parallel. But line 68 fetches commits sequentially after the parallel batch completes. The commits fetch is independent of the other three and could be included in the Promise.all.

issues.summary: Project data arrays imported independently by both +page.server.ts and +page.svelte, creating parallel consumption paths
issues.suggestion: Have +page.server.ts return { impactProjects: Project[], explorationProjects: Project[], metaProjects: Project[] } with GitHub data already attached, eliminating the need for +page.svelte to import from lib/data/projects and removing the attachGithubData helper.
evidence: +page.server.ts imports impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects to extract repo names for GitHub fetching. +page.svelte independently imports the same three arrays to render project sections. The server could return categorized projects with GitHub data attached, eliminating the client-side attachGithubData function and the duplicate import.

issues.summary: Terminal.svelte uses onMount() while +page.svelte uses $effect() for equivalent IntersectionObserver setup
issues.suggestion: Migrate Terminal.svelte from onMount() to $effect() to match the runes-based lifecycle pattern used in +page.svelte, which aligns with the project's stated Svelte 5 runes convention in CLAUDE.md.
evidence: Terminal.svelte line 17: onMount(() => { ... new IntersectionObserver ... }) uses the Svelte 4 lifecycle import
+page.svelte line 40: $effect(() => { ... new IntersectionObserver ... }) uses the Svelte 5 rune for equivalent observer setup
